lesson_title: Configuring your compiler: Warning and error levels

cards.front: What is the difference between a compiler warning and a compiler error?
cards.back: Errors prevent compilation - the code won't compile. Warnings indicate potential problems but the code will still compile. Warnings often point to bugs or bad practices.
cards.source: 0.11 - Configuring your compiler: Warning and error levels

cards.text: Compiler {{c1::errors}} prevent compilation, while {{c2::warnings}} indicate potential problems but allow compilation to continue.
cards.source: 0.11 - Configuring your compiler: Warning and error levels

cards.front: Why should you fix all compiler warnings?
cards.back: Warnings usually indicate real bugs or code smell. Even if the code works now, warnings often lead to bugs later. It's much easier to fix warnings immediately than debug mysterious problems later.
cards.source: 0.11 - Configuring your compiler: Warning and error levels

cards.concept: Why you should enable all warnings and treat them as errors
cards.explanation:
Maximizing warnings catches problems early:
  • Warnings often indicate real bugs (uninitialized variables, type mismatches)
  • Treating warnings as errors forces you to fix them
  • Prevents warning fatigue (ignoring warnings until a real one is missed)
  • Higher warning levels catch subtle bugs
  • Clean warning-free code is more maintainable
Professional projects always enable maximum warnings.
examples: Warning: unused variable → might be a typo or logic error
Warning: implicit conversion → potential data loss
Warning: comparison of signed/unsigned → can cause bugs
cards.source: 0.11 - Configuring your compiler: Warning and error levels

cards.text: In Visual Studio, set warning level to {{c1::/W4}} or {{c2::/Wall}} and enable {{c3::/WX}} to treat warnings as errors.
cards.source: 0.11 - Configuring your compiler: Warning and error levels

cards.front: What do the Visual Studio warning level flags /W0 through /W4 mean?
cards.back: /W0 = no warnings, /W1 = severe warnings only, /W2 = level 1 + more, /W3 = recommended (default), /W4 = all reasonable warnings (recommended for new code).
cards.source: 0.11 - Configuring your compiler: Warning and error levels

cards.text: In GCC/Clang, use {{c1::-Wall -Wextra}} to enable most warnings and {{c2::-Werror}} to treat warnings as errors.
cards.source: 0.11 - Configuring your compiler: Warning and error levels

cards.front: What warnings do -Wall and -Wextra enable in GCC/Clang?
cards.back: -Wall enables common important warnings (despite the name, not all warnings). -Wextra enables additional warnings that are less common but still valuable.
cards.source: 0.11 - Configuring your compiler: Warning and error levels

cards.front: What is the /WX flag (or -Werror) and why use it?
cards.back: /WX or -Werror treats all warnings as errors, causing compilation to fail. This forces you to fix warnings immediately rather than accumulating technical debt.
cards.source: 0.11 - Configuring your compiler: Warning and error levels

cards.question: What warning would this code likely generate?
cards.answer: Warning: variable 'x' is uninitialized when used
cards.explanation: Using uninitialized variables is undefined behavior. A good warning level will catch this common mistake.
cards.source: 0.11 - Configuring your compiler: Warning and error levels

cards.explanation:
Professional development maintains zero warnings:
  1. Enable maximum warning levels from day one
  2. Treat warnings as errors (/WX or -Werror)
  3. Fix warnings immediately as they appear
  4. Never commit code with warnings
  5. Use warning suppressions sparingly and document why
Zero-warning policy prevents warning fatigue and catches bugs early.
examples: Configure warnings in project settings, not per-file
Code review: reject PRs with warnings
CI/CD: build fails on any warning
cards.source: 0.11 - Configuring your compiler: Warning and error levels

cards.front: When is it acceptable to suppress a specific compiler warning?
cards.back: Only when you're certain the warning is a false positive and you've documented why. Suppression should be rare, localized, and always explained with comments.
cards.source: 0.11 - Configuring your compiler: Warning and error levels
